title: Congee with Century Egg

seo.title: Authentic Congee with Century Egg Recipe | Easy Chinese Breakfast

seo.imageAlt: A bowl of creamy Congee with Century Egg garnished with green onions and ginger, a traditional Chinese breakfast.
seo.imageCaption: Warm up with a comforting bowl of Congee with Century Egg, a classic Chinese breakfast that's both hearty and flavorful.

description: Congee with Century Egg is a quintessential Chinese breakfast, offering a warm and comforting start to your day. This savory rice porridge is slowly cooked to a silky smooth consistency, infused with the unique, umami flavor of diced century eggs and often complemented by tender pork or ginger. It is a hearty and deeply satisfying dish cherished for its delicate texture and rich, complex taste.

ingredients.notes: peeled and finely julienned or minced

instructions.title: Prepare the Rice
instructions.text: Rinse the jasmine rice thoroughly under cold running water until the water runs clear. Soak the rice in a bowl of water for at least 30 minutes, or up to 2 hours. This helps the rice cook faster and achieve a creamier texture. Drain the soaked rice before cooking.

instructions.title: Start Cooking the Congee Base
instructions.text: In a large pot with a heavy bottom, combine the drained rice and 8 cups of water or chicken broth. Bring the mixture to a rolling boil over high heat, stirring occasionally to prevent sticking. Once boiling, reduce the heat to low, cover the pot partially, and let it simmer.

instructions.title: Prepare Century Eggs and Pork
instructions.text: While the congee simmers, peel the century eggs and dice them into small, bite-sized pieces. If using pork, slice or shred it thinly. Prepare the fresh ginger by peeling and julienning or mincing it finely. Slice the green onions for garnish.

instructions.title: Add Ingredients and Simmer
instructions.text: After the congee has simmered for about 20-25 minutes, the rice grains should have broken down and started to thicken the liquid. Add the diced century eggs, julienned ginger, and pork (if using) to the pot. Stir well to combine.

instructions.title: Achieve Desired Consistency
instructions.text: Continue to simmer the congee for another 10-15 minutes, stirring frequently to prevent the bottom from scorching. If the congee becomes too thick, add the remaining 1-2 cups of water or broth gradually until it reaches your preferred creamy consistency. The rice should be fully broken down and resemble a smooth, thick porridge.

instructions.title: Season and Serve
instructions.text: Season the congee generously with salt and white pepper to taste. Ladle the hot congee into individual serving bowls. Garnish each bowl with fresh sliced green onions and a drizzle of sesame oil, if desired. Serve immediately for a comforting Chinese breakfast.

tips: For extra creamy congee, freeze the soaked rice overnight before cooking. The freezing process helps break down the rice grains, resulting in a smoother texture.
Do not over-stir the congee initially, as this can make it gluey. Stir more frequently towards the end of cooking to prevent sticking and ensure even cooking.
Adjust the amount of water or broth to achieve your desired consistency. Some prefer a thicker congee, while others like it looser. Always add liquid gradually.
article:
Embrace the comforting embrace of a warm bowl of congee, a staple in Chinese households and a beloved breakfast across Asia. While simple rice porridge forms its base, the magic truly unfolds with a myriad of flavorful additions. Among the most iconic and satisfying variations is Congee with Century Egg, a dish that masterfully balances creamy texture with unique, savory notes. This comprehensive guide will walk you through everything you need to know to recreate this classic Chinese breakfast at home, transforming humble ingredients into a truly delightful meal.

Often enjoyed for its soothing qualities and easy digestibility, congee is more than just food; it’s a bowl of warmth and tradition. When paired with the distinctive taste of century eggs and often tender shredded pork, it becomes a hearty and flavorful start to any day, or a comforting meal at any time.

## What is Congee with Century Egg?

Congee, or jook as it’s known in Cantonese, is a savory rice porridge made by simmering rice in a large amount of water or broth until the grains break down into a thick, creamy consistency. It’s a versatile dish, often served plain for those feeling under the weather, or dressed up with various toppings and ingredients to create a more substantial meal.

The star of this particular variation is the century egg (pidan), a preserved duck, chicken, or quail egg that has been cured in a mixture of clay, ash, salt, quicklime, and rice hulls for several weeks or months. Its appearance is striking: the albumen turns into a translucent, dark brown jelly, and the yolk becomes a dark green or gray paste with a creamy texture and a distinct, savory, umami-rich flavor that some describe as earthy or slightly ammonia-like. Don't let its unique look deter you; when diced and added to hot congee, the century egg provides a depth of flavor and a silky mouthfeel that is truly addictive. Often, this dish is further enhanced with shredded lean pork, fresh ginger, and a sprinkle of green onions, creating the beloved Congee with Century Egg and Shredded Pork (皮蛋瘦肉粥).

## Ingredients for Congee with Century Egg

To create a truly authentic and delicious bowl of Congee with Century Egg, you'll need the following ingredients:

*   **For the Congee Base:**
    *   1 cup long-grain jasmine rice (or short-grain for a creamier texture)
    *   8-10 cups water or chicken broth (broth adds more flavor)
    *   1 teaspoon salt, plus more to taste
    *   1/2 teaspoon white pepper
*   **For the Toppings & Flavor:**
    *   2-3 century eggs (pidan), peeled and diced
    *   4 oz lean pork shoulder or loin, shredded (optional, but highly recommended)
    *   1-inch piece of fresh ginger, peeled and julienned
    *   2 tablespoons chopped green onions, for garnish
    *   1 teaspoon sesame oil, for drizzling
*   **For Marinating Pork (if using):**
    *   1 teaspoon soy sauce
    *   1/2 teaspoon cornstarch
    *   1/2 teaspoon Shaoxing wine (optional)
    *   Pinch of white pepper

## Step-by-Step Instructions to Make Congee

Making Congee with Century Egg is a labor of love, but it's largely hands-off and incredibly rewarding.

1.  **Prepare the Rice:** Rinse the rice thoroughly under cold running water until the water runs clear. This removes excess starch and prevents the congee from becoming too gummy. For an even creamier congee and faster cooking time, you can soak the rice in water for at least 30 minutes, or even overnight, then drain.
2.  **Marinate the Pork (if using):** If you're adding pork, shred it into thin strips. In a small bowl, combine the shredded pork with soy sauce, cornstarch, Shaoxing wine (if using), and white pepper. Mix well and set aside to marinate for at least 15 minutes.
3.  **Prepare Century Eggs:** Carefully peel the century eggs. They can be a bit tricky, so peel them under running water if needed. Once peeled, dice them into small, bite-sized pieces. Set aside.
4.  **Start the Congee:** In a large, heavy-bottomed pot, combine the rinsed rice and 8 cups of water or chicken broth. Bring the mixture to a rolling boil over high heat, stirring occasionally to prevent the rice from sticking to the bottom.
5.  **Simmer and Stir:** Once boiling, reduce the heat to low, cover the pot partially (leaving a small gap for steam to escape), and let it simmer. Stir every 15-20 minutes, scraping the bottom of the pot to prevent sticking. This slow simmering process allows the rice grains to break down and release their starch, creating the signature creamy texture. This will take anywhere from 1.5 to 2 hours.
6.  **Add Pork and Ginger:** After about 1 hour of simmering, the rice grains should have started to bloom. Add the marinated shredded pork and julienned ginger to the congee. Stir well and continue to simmer. The pork will cook quickly in the hot congee.
7.  **Adjust Consistency:** As the congee cooks, it will thicken significantly. If it becomes too thick for your liking, add the remaining 1-2 cups of water or broth, a little at a time, until you reach your desired consistency. Remember, it will thicken further as it cools.
8.  **Add Century Eggs and Season:** About 10-15 minutes before serving, stir in the diced century eggs. Simmer briefly to warm them through. Season the Congee with Century Egg with salt and white pepper to taste. A good congee should be well-seasoned.
9.  **Serve:** Ladle the hot congee into individual bowls. Drizzle with a teaspoon of sesame oil and garnish generously with chopped green onions.

## Pro Tips for Perfect Congee with Century Egg

*   **Rinse and Soak Your Rice:** Rinsing prevents gumminess, and soaking significantly reduces cooking time while promoting a creamier texture.
*   **Use Broth for Flavor:** While water works, chicken broth adds a wonderful depth of umami that elevates the congee.
*   **Don't Rush It:** True congee takes time. Low and slow simmering is key to achieving that perfectly broken-down, creamy consistency.
*   **Stir Regularly:** Especially in the beginning and towards the end, stir often to prevent the rice from sticking to the bottom of the pot and burning.
*   **Adjust Consistency as You Go:** Don't be afraid to add more hot water or broth if the congee becomes too thick. It should be thick but pourable.
*   **Add Century Eggs Towards the End:** Overcooking century eggs can diminish their unique texture and flavor. Add them in the last 10-15 minutes of cooking just to warm them through.
*   **Taste and Adjust Seasoning:** Salt and white pepper are crucial. Taste the congee before serving and adjust as needed.

## Serving Suggestions & Pairings

Congee with Century Egg is a complete meal on its own, but it truly shines when accompanied by a few traditional Chinese side dishes.

*   **Youtiao (Chinese Fried Dough Sticks):** These crispy, slightly salty dough sticks are perfect for dipping into the hot congee, soaking up all the flavorful broth.
*   **Pickled Vegetables:** A small plate of preserved radish, pickled mustard greens, or spicy fermented tofu provides a refreshing textural contrast and a zingy flavor that cuts through the richness of the congee.
*   **Extra Condiments:** Offer soy sauce, a dash of chili oil for those who like a kick, or even some crispy fried shallots or garlic for added texture and aroma.
*   **Fresh Herbs:** Beyond green onions, a sprinkle of fresh cilantro can add a burst of freshness.

Serve your Congee with Century Egg piping hot for the ultimate comforting experience.

## Variations and Substitutions

While Congee with Century Egg is a classic, there are many ways to adapt it to your preferences or what you have on hand.

*   **Protein Alternatives:** Instead of pork, you can use shredded chicken breast, thinly sliced fish fillets (add them in the last 10 minutes of cooking), or even dried scallops (soaked and shredded for extra umami). For a vegetarian version, omit the pork and add sliced mushrooms (shiitake or oyster work well) along with extra ginger.
*   **Egg Variations:** If century eggs are hard to find or not to your taste, you can substitute with salted duck eggs (also preserved, but with a different flavor profile) or even regular hard-boiled eggs, sliced and added as a topping.
*   **Rice Types:** While jasmine rice is standard, you can experiment with short-grain sushi rice for a stickier, creamier texture, or even brown rice for a nuttier flavor and added fiber (though it will require a longer cooking time and more liquid).
*   **Flavor Boosters:** Consider adding a few dried shiitake mushrooms (rehydrated and sliced) along with the pork for an earthy depth, or a splash of fish sauce for an extra layer of savory flavor.
*   **Vegetarian Congee:** Use vegetable broth instead of chicken broth. Incorporate ingredients like rehydrated dried shiitake mushrooms, carrots, corn, or even tofu for a hearty vegetarian option.

## Frequently Asked Questions

*   **What is a century egg?** A century egg is a preserved egg (usually duck, sometimes chicken or quail) that undergoes a curing process in a mixture of clay, ash, salt, quicklime, and rice hulls. This process transforms the yolk into a dark green, creamy paste and the albumen into a translucent, dark brown jelly with a unique savory flavor.
*   **Can I make congee in a slow cooker?** Absolutely! A slow cooker is excellent for congee. Combine rinsed rice and liquid (ratio typically 1 cup rice to 8-10 cups liquid) and cook on low for 6-8 hours or on high for 3-4 hours, stirring occasionally. Add pork and ginger in the last hour, and century eggs in the last 30 minutes.
*   **How long does congee last?** Leftover congee can be stored in an airtight container in the refrigerator for 2-3 days. It will thicken significantly as it cools; simply reheat on the stovetop with a splash of water or broth to restore its desired consistency.
*   **Is Congee with Century Egg healthy?** Congee is generally considered a light and easily digestible meal, often eaten when one is unwell. The century egg adds protein and unique nutrients. It can be a very healthy and comforting option, especially if made with lean protein and plenty of ginger.
*   **Can I use leftover cooked rice to make congee?** Yes, you can! Using leftover cooked rice significantly reduces the cooking time. For 1 cup of cooked rice, you'll need about 4-6 cups of liquid. Simmer for about 30-45 minutes, stirring frequently, until it reaches the desired consistency. The texture might be slightly different from congee made with raw rice, but it’s a great shortcut.

Congee with Century Egg is more than just a dish; it's an experience of comfort, tradition, and unique flavors. Its creamy texture and savory depth make it an unforgettable part of Chinese culinary heritage. Whether you're seeking a hearty breakfast, a soothing meal, or simply an adventure into authentic Chinese cuisine, this recipe offers a rewarding journey. Don't hesitate to try making this wonderful dish at home and discover the magic of century eggs yourself!
